1:
The chapter opens on the heels of Absalom's fall, and the witnesses read the timing as ominous providence. John Gill insists that though Sheba was there "with respect to second causes" by chance, God so ordered it "for the further fulfilment of the threatening to David, that the sword should not depart from his house." Matthew Henry presses the same lesson pastorally: the end of one trouble is often the beginning of another, and a reconciliation newly made must be handled tenderly, "lest the peace break again before it be settled. A broken bone, when it is set, must have time to knot."

The grammatical-historical commentators fix the man and his cry. Charles Ellicott notes that the English, following the ancient versions, takes Bichri as a proper name, but "most recent commentators consider it as a patronymic, the Bichrite," of the family of Becher, second son of Benjamin, so that Sheba is "to this extent at least, of the same clan with Saul." His shout, "every man to his tents," Ellicott compares to Jeroboam's later signal of revolt (1 Kings 12:16). Adam Clarke agrees he was a Benjamite who "probably belonged to the family of Saul," and paraphrases the slogan: we of Israel are under no obligation to the house of David.

Henry traces the whole rising to the foolish quarrel of the previous chapter, the elders of Israel and Judah disputing who had the larger share in the king, and draws the political moral that it is as "impolitic for princes to be partial... as it is for parents" to favour one child. He hears in the tribes' swing from "we have ten parts in David" to "we have no part in him" the fickleness of the crowd: "Today Hosanna, to-morrow Crucify." John Wesley adds the note of contempt in the rebel's phrase "the son of Jesse," reducing the anointed king to a mere private person. Beneath their differences of angle the voices agree: the Benjamite grudge of Saul's house was hereditary, and against David's throne, a type (Henry says) of the Messiah's kingdom, there is a standing enmity that "he that sits in heaven" laughs to scorn (Ps. 2:1-2).
2:
The single verse records a hinge in David's fortunes, and the witnesses dwell on its irony. Keil and Delitzsch parse the Hebrew closely: the men of Israel "went up (to the mountains) away from David and after Sheba," while the men of Judah "clave unto their king, from the Jordan to Jerusalem." They note that the construction of dabaq ("clave") with the preposition is "a pregnant one: they adhered to and followed him," and explain that "from Jordan" need not mean the revolt broke out at the river, but only that Judah had already fetched the king back across it.

Charles Ellicott draws out the reversal that governs the whole episode. David's negotiations had produced "an entire reversal of the position of the tribes": Judah, "among whom the rebellion originated, and who had been tardy in returning to their allegiance, were now fierce in their loyalty," while Israel, who had first proposed bringing David home, "had become alienated and rebellious." The very tribe most guilty under Absalom is now most devoted; the tribes most recently loyal now defect.

John Gill and John Wesley temper the sweep of the language. Gill observes soberly that "those that met him on the road departed from him," and Wesley glosses "every man of Israel" as "the generality of those Israelites who were present," not literally all, but the crowd swept along by a sudden cry. Together the witnesses make the verse a study in the instability of popular favour: the same multitude that had strewn the king's path with loyalty flows off after a lawless Benjamite at the first offence to their pride. What holds Judah steady is not superior virtue but the mending of a bruised affection; what carries Israel away is the same wounded honour that, an hour before, had made them clamour to bring the king back. The nearness of these two crowds, one cleaving and one deserting, warns how thin is the line between the returning subject and the rebel, and how little the shouting of a multitude can be trusted by any who lean upon it. Ellicott's contrast becomes the moral of the verse: loyalty and revolt are not fixed properties of the tribes but moods of an offended pride, and the king who a day earlier could not tell friend from foe now watches the whole nation sort itself, for the moment, by nothing steadier than which grievance stings the sharper.
3:
David's return to Jerusalem brings the melancholy business of the ten concubines, and the witnesses treat it with grave restraint. Keil and Delitzsch follow the Hebrew: as soon as the king reached his palace he brought the ten women "whom he had left behind, and with whom Absalom had lain, into a place of safety, and took care of them, without going in unto them any more." They render the closing phrase as "in widowhood of life," an adverbial accusative meaning lifelong widowhood "until the day of their death." Charles Ellicott weighs the same clause and offers the two readings: either "in widowhood of life," as the English has it, "or 'in widowhood of the living,' i.e., while their husband was living, as in the Chaldee" — widows, that is, of a man not dead.

Adam Clarke lays out David's dilemma with a lawyer's economy: "he could not well divorce them; he could not punish them, as they were not in the transgression; he could no more be familiar with them, because they had been defiled by his son; and to have married them to other men might have been dangerous to the state." The one course left was to "shut them up and fed them, made them quite comfortable, and they continued as widows to their death." John Gill supplies only the setting, David's palace "in that part of the city called the fort of Zion, and city of David."

Ellicott names the deeper cause without flinching: this melancholy result was "the necessary result, under the system of polygamy, of what had happened." Here the witnesses converge with Matthew Henry's judgement on the same women (offered under the opening verse), that David "had multiplied wives, contrary to the law, and they proved a grief and shame to him." Those he had "sinfully taken pleasure in" he was now obliged in duty, prudence, and justice alike to put away. The verse becomes a quiet parable of sin's long entail: the king's early self-indulgence ripens, a generation later, into an incurable sorrow, and ten women pass their lives in a living widowhood that no repentance of David's can now undo.
4:
David's first act against the new revolt is to commission Amasa, and the witnesses are unanimous that it was a well-meant blunder. Keil and Delitzsch explain that the order to "assemble me the men of Judah within three days" was "the commencement of the fulfilment of the promise" David had made Amasa (2 Sam. 19:13) to set him over the army in Joab's room. Yet they call it "a very imprudent act, like the promise itself," since Joab, "who had been commander of the army for so many years, was grievously offended by it," and had "incomparably more distinction in the tribe of Judah than Amasa," who "had even led the rebel army." Charles Ellicott agrees in a phrase: "It proved an act of very doubtful expediency at this crisis."

Matthew Henry takes the longer view, headlining "Amasa's fall just as he began to rise." He had been "Absalom's general," came over to David "upon a promise that he should be general," and Sheba's revolt gave David "occasion to fulfil his promise sooner than he could wish." But the fulfilment, crossed by "Joab's envy and emulation," proved "of ill consequence both to him and David." Henry notes shrewdly that the men of Judah, "though forward to attend the king's triumphs, were backward enough to fight his battles," and moralizes that "most love a loyalty, as well as a religion, that is cheap and easy. Many boast of their being akin to Christ that yet are very loth to venture for him."

Adam Clarke reads the commission as an investiture: David "invests him with the command of the army," sending him to gather Judah "and to come back to receive his orders... in three days," while John Gill notes only that David thereby "declared him" general, as he had promised. The verse thus gathers the moral tension of the whole chapter: David keeps faith with a former enemy, and clemency toward Amasa is real; but by preferring an untried turncoat over a proven and dangerous soldier he lights the fuse that will explode, one verse later, in Amasa's blood. Good intentions, the witnesses warn, do not exempt a king from the consequences of imprudence.
5:
Amasa's delay is the pivot of the tragedy, and the witnesses probe both its text and its cause. Keil and Delitzsch attend first to the words, noting the variant between the written text and the margin (Chethib and Keri) in the verb "he tarried," and judge the correction "quite unnecessary." They then read the delay as the trigger of David's alarm: fearing Sheba would prove "more injurious (more dangerous) to us than Absalom," the king turns to Abishai, urging pursuit "lest he reach fortified cities, and tear out our eye," the eye being, as in Deut. 32:10 and Zech. 2:12, the figure for "the most valuable possession."

Why Amasa tarried is a genuine and unresolved question among the witnesses. Charles Ellicott confesses that "no cause is assigned," and reasons from the fact that Amasa is later "met on his return at Gibeon" (v. 8) that "he had therefore gone quite out of the bounds of Judah into Benjamin, and had consumed more time in consequence of exceeding his instructions" — a fact that "suggests great doubt of his fitness for the place David had promised him," and that Joab will later exploit by insinuating "that Amasa was not really loyal." John Wesley offers two humbler explanations: either "the people being wearied out by the late war, were not forward to engage in another," or "the soldiers had more affection to Joab, than to their new general." Keil combines the guesses, supposing "the men of Judah distrusted him, and were not very ready to respond to his summons."

The disagreement is instructive rather than idle. If Amasa exceeded his orders (Ellicott), the fault is his own incapacity; if the men held back (Wesley, Keil), the fault lies in a soldiery still bound to Joab. Either way the witnesses converge on the dramatic point: the appointed general cannot muster his troops in time, and into that fatal gap steps the man he displaced. The verse is the hinge on which the whole grim sequence turns, and David's own words, ranking Sheba above Absalom, betray how gravely a divided army endangers the crown.
6:
David's turn to Abishai rather than Joab is the detail the witnesses seize on, for it reveals the king's mind. John Gill is explicit: "it seems he would have nothing to say to Joab, being displeased with him for slaying Absalom, and having removed him from his posts; and therefore speaks to the next officer in his army, Abishai." Gill even corrects the historian, noting that "Josephus says, he addressed himself to Joab, contrary to the express words of the text." Charles Ellicott reads the same purpose: "David is determined to pass over Joab, and, therefore, when Amasa fails in this crisis... he summons Abishai," putting him "in command of such forces as were at hand in Jerusalem" for "the rapid pursuit of Sheba."

John Wesley supplies the tactful reasoning behind the snub: David spoke "not to Joab; lest by this means he should recover his place, and Amasa be discontented, and David's fidelity in making good his promise to Amasa be questioned." The king is trying, even now, to keep faith with the man he has raised, and to keep the man he has demoted at arm's length.

The commentators also linger over the urgency and the obscurity of David's warning. He fears Sheba may "get him fenced cities, and escape us," and the final clause is a knot the witnesses cannot quite untie. Keil and Delitzsch render it, from the previous verse, "tear out our eye," meaning "do us a serious injury." Ellicott lists the rival renderings: "the English follows the Vulg[ate]"; others translate "pluck out our eye," that is, "do us great harm"; others follow the Septuagint's "overshadow our eye," meaning either "cause us anxiety, or hide where we cannot find him." Beneath the philological uncertainty the sense is agreed and vivid: a rebel who reaches walled cities becomes a running sore in the body politic. David's whole strategy is speed, and his willingness to command through Abishai rather than swallow his pride and recall Joab shows a king who has learned to distrust his most capable and most dangerous servant, even at the cost of efficiency.
7:
The muster that marches out of Jerusalem is described in a single crowded verse, and the witnesses use it chiefly to identify the forces and to note the ominous presence of Joab. Charles Ellicott catalogues them: "Joab's men" are "the body of men who were usually under Joab's immediate command, and who would readily follow his brother"; the Cherethites and Pelethites he refers back to his note at 2 Sam. 8:18; and "the mighty men" he identifies with the corps of heroes in 2 Sam. 23:8, "probably made up chiefly of those who had been with David in his life as an outlaw." John Gill makes the same quiet observation about Joab's regiment: "though he was removed from his post as general, yet might still have the command of a regiment."

The telling point, for the witnesses, is that Abishai is nominally in charge but Joab is in the ranks. John Wesley states it bluntly: these were "the remainders of Joab's army who were there present, with whom also Joab might go as a reformade, watching an opportunity to do what he designed." The military term Wesley borrows, a "reformade," an officer serving without his command, exactly captures the sinister position: the deposed general rides out among troops that are his in all but title, biding his time.

Read together the excerpts turn a bare list into a study in latent menace. David has entrusted the pursuit to Abishai and left Joab officially out; but the men are Joab's men, the household guard is loyal to the house that has led them since the wilderness years, and Joab himself is present, armed, and calculating. The verse thus loads the coming meeting at Gibeon with dramatic inevitability. All the pieces of the murder are already on the road, and the reader who has followed the witnesses knows, before Amasa ever appears, whose hand will be quickest when the two columns meet. The soldiers march for David; but the man who commands their hearts marches for himself. It is a sobering thing, the commentators imply, that the king could depose Joab from his rank yet not from his hold upon the army; the title had changed hands, but the loyalty of the mighty men, forged in the years of exile, had not, and a general so loved is more dangerous demoted than in office, for he keeps the substance of command while shedding its accountability.
8:
At the great stone in Gibeon the columns meet, and the witnesses give unusual attention to the mechanics of Joab's dress, for on that hinge the murder turns. John Gill sets the scene, noting from Josephus that Gibeon lay "forty furlongs, or five miles from Jerusalem," and that "this place was appointed for the rendezvous of David's forces," where Amasa "came... and joined them, and took the command." Keil and Delitzsch unravel the tangled Hebrew, showing that the verse is a chain of "circumstantial clauses" describing how "the girdle of the sword was bound over it upon his loins in its sheath, which came out, and it fell," and they register their surprise "that Joab appears among them... as the commander of the forces."

The crux is the sword, and here two readings meet. Adam Clarke supposes the fall accidental: Joab wore "no arms but a short sword... so loose in its sheath that it could be easily drawn out," and thinks he actually "stumbled" (so "some of the versions, and able critics, understand the words it fell out"), took up the sword "with his left hand as if he had no bad intention," and then, "taking Amasa by the beard with his right hand, pretending to kiss him," struck. Charles Ellicott and John Wesley read the fall as contrived: Ellicott says Joab "had stuck a dagger so arranged that it might fall out as he advanced," and "then picked this up naturally in his left hand," so that "his movements gave rise to no suspicion"; Wesley agrees that "things having... been so contrived by Joab, that upon the least motion of his body, his sword should drop out."

Whether accident or artifice, the witnesses agree on the effect Ellicott names: "the object of this verse is to explain how Joab... was able to stab Amasa without his purpose being suspected." Clarke adds the cultural touch that "in the Eastern country it is the beard, not the man, which is usually kissed," so that the coming embrace would rouse no alarm. The evangelists of the text thus build, clause by clause, a portrait of premeditation: the plain coat, the loosened blade, the outstretched hand of greeting, all arranged so that friendship itself becomes the weapon's cover.
9:
The verse holds the moment of the embrace, and the witnesses read it as the very picture of treachery smiling. John Gill catches the false warmth of Joab's greeting, "Art thou in health, my brother?", spoken, he says, "in a friendly manner, with all the air of pleasantry and good humour." The geniality is total and the malice is total, and the two are worn as one face.

Keil and Delitzsch supply the ethnographic detail that makes the scene intelligible: Joab "laid hold of his beard with his right hand to kiss him," and "laying hold of the beard to kiss is still customary among Arabs and Turks as a sign of friendly welcome." The gesture that to Amasa meant kinship and peace was, in Joab's hand, the means of drawing his cousin close and holding him still. Keil states the motive without qualification: "the reason for this assassination was Joab's jealousy of Amasa," the resentment of a displaced commander toward the man set over him.

The witnesses do not debate here; they contemplate. What arrests them is the marriage of intimacy and murder, the way every token of affection, the brother's name, the question after health, the hand at the beard, the offered kiss, is bent into an instrument of death. Matthew Henry, commenting on the whole scene under the fourth verse, names what the others imply: Joab "did it treacherously, and under pretence of friendship, that Amasa might not be upon his guard. He called him brother... and took him by the beard, as one he was free with, to kiss him, while with the drawn sword in his other hand he was aiming at his heart." The verse thus becomes, for the historic church, a standing emblem of the counterfeit friendship that Scripture everywhere warns against, the smooth word that covers a violent heart, the kiss that is the nearer for being false. Amasa's fatal error, the commentators agree, was not folly but trust: he read the beard and the brotherly word at face value, and had no category for a welcome that was a trap. That very openness, which in another setting would be a virtue, becomes here the occasion of his death, and the witnesses leave the reader to weigh how often the innocent are undone precisely because they will not suspect in others the treachery of which they are themselves incapable.
10:
The blow falls, and the witnesses read both the deed and the strange passivity of Amasa. John Gill explains why the victim did not defend himself: "he might see the sword drop out of the scabbard, and Joab take it up, which he supposed he did in order to put it into its sheath again, having no suspicion of his wicked design, and therefore not at all upon his guard." John Wesley reads the moment identically, that Amasa "supposed that Joab intended only to put it into its scabbard, and therefore took no care to defend himself against the stroke."

On the wound itself the witnesses correct the familiar phrase. Adam Clarke argues that the word rendered "the fifth rib" means rather "any part of the abdominal region"; the Septuagint has "the groin," the Targum "the thigh," and "that it means some part of the abdominal region, is evident from what follows, And shed out his bowels to the ground." He concludes with grim plainness: "in plain English, he ripped up his belly." Charles Ellicott agrees, glossing "fifth rib" simply as "abdomen," and cross-referencing the identical death of Abner (2 Sam. 2:23). Keil and Delitzsch note the phrase "he smote him... and repeated not (the stroke)," one steady thrust that needed no second, and add the flat verdict: "the reason for this assassination was Joab's jealousy."

The witnesses then turn to a practical puzzle: why did Amasa's own men not avenge him? Wesley answers with characteristic shrewdness that "not many of them were yet come up," and that "Joab's interest and authority with the military-men was very great, especially with David's guards, who were here present, and who had no kindness for Amasa, as having been the general of the rebellious army." Ellicott adds that Joab now "assumes his old place as commander-in-chief, doubtless with the connivance of his brother." The murder is thus not only a private revenge but a coup: with one unrepeated stroke Joab removes his rival, resumes his command, and marches on after Sheba as though nothing had happened, the same man, the witnesses remember, who had shed Abner's blood and "went unpunished for it."
11:
Joab does not linger by the corpse; he leaves a herald, and the witnesses read the moment as a masterstroke of ruthless propaganda. John Gill explains the arrangement: one of Joab's men stood by the body, "no doubt by the order of Joab, to satisfy the people as they came up, and reconcile them to this fact, and to exhort them not to stop, but to follow after Joab; for though Amasa their general was dead, Joab had taken the command." Adam Clarke paraphrases the cry: "There is now no other commander besides Joab; and Joab is steadily attached to David: let those therefore who are loyal follow Joab."

John Wesley unfolds the two clauses of the slogan. "He that favoureth Joab" means "he that would have Joab to be general, rather than such a perfidious rebel as Amasa," while "he that is for David" means "he that wisheth David good success against Sheba, and against all rebels." The genius of the appeal, the witnesses see, is that it yokes Joab's cause to David's so tightly that the soldier who is loyal to his king is made to endorse, in the same breath, the murder of his king's appointed general.

Charles Ellicott exposes the cynicism most fully. "Time was too precious for Joab himself to wait"; he must "put down the rebellion of Sheba by rapid action, and thereby render himself impregnable in the high office... which he had now again usurped." He therefore left "one of his trusty men... by the body of Amasa, with a battle cry which should suggest that he had rightly been put to death for his doubtful loyalty." And Ellicott names the true spring of it all: "Joab's real motive for murdering Amasa, as for murdering Abner (2 Sam. 3:27), was personal jealousy and ambition." The witnesses thus let the verse stand as a lesson in how easily a private crime dresses itself in the language of public loyalty, so that men are recruited to a murderer's banner under the honest colours of their king. There is a bitter cunning in the timing: Joab does not pause to justify himself or to bury his victim, but converts the very shock of the killing into an argument, letting the freshly spilt blood recommend him as the only man decisive enough to finish the war. The soldiers, offered a simple binary of Joab or the rebel, choose as he intends, and the crime is ratified not by any court but by the marching feet of an army that would rather follow a strong hand than pause to ask by what right it holds the sword.
12:
The verse fixes on the gruesome obstacle of Amasa's body in the road, and the witnesses read both the physical fact and its moral weight. John Gill observes that "though the wound was mortal... as yet there was life in him, and through the pain he was in, and the pangs of death on him, he rolled himself about in his own blood in the high road." Adam Clarke confirms the horror from experience: "it is very likely that Amasa did not immediately die; I have known instances of persons living several hours after their bowels had been shed out."

Keil and Delitzsch and Charles Ellicott explain the crowd's paralysis. The soldiers coming up "stood still (by the corpse)," and Ellicott suggests these "were probably the very people whom Amasa had just been gathering from Judah and Benjamin," "naturally overcome and paralysed for the moment at the sight of the great leader whom the king had just promoted wallowing in his blood." So Joab's man "turned (pushed) Amasa from the road to the field, and threw a cloth over him," whereupon "they all passed by and went after Joab." The removal was pure expedience, clearing the road so the march could resume.

John Wesley reads the same actions but presses them toward judgement. The people "stood still," he says, "wondering at the spectacle, and enquiring into the author and occasion of it"; the body was removed because the sight "both incensed them against Joab and hindered the king's service"; and the cloth was thrown over it to hide the crime. But Wesley will not let the concealment stand as final: "the covering of blood with a cloth cannot stop its cry to God for vengeance." Here he joins Matthew Henry, who on the same scene wrote that "wicked men think themselves safe in their wickedness if they can but conceal it from the eye of the world," yet "the covering of blood with a cloth cannot stop its cry in God's ear." The witnesses thus turn a soldier's tidy expedient into a parable: men may drag a crime out of the highway and spread a cloth upon it, but blood shed unjustly has a voice that no covering silences, and it speaks to heaven though the army marches on.
13:
This brief transitional verse records only that, once the body was out of the way, "all the people went on after Joab, to pursue after Sheba," and the witnesses treat it as the quiet resumption of the march. John Gill marks the single condition that freed the road: Amasa was "removed out of the highway... into the field, and covered over with a cloth, that he could not be seen." Once the object of the soldiers' horror was hidden, the column that had halted in shock could move again.

Keil and Delitzsch use the moment to carry the narrative forward and to fix the geography, joining this verse to the next: Joab "went through all the tribes of Israel to Abela, and Beth-maacah," a place they identify with "the large Christian village of Abil... called Abil-el-Kamh on account of its excellent wheat," lying "to the north-west of Lake Huleh." The pursuit thus reaches from the neighbourhood of Gibeon to the far northern border of the land.

The witnesses draw out what the bare sentence implies. The whole horror of Amasa's murder has been reduced, in the eyes of the marching army, to a delay of minutes, an obstacle in the road removed and forgotten. The men who a moment before stood transfixed now stream after the very man whose hand is red, because his voice is the one that commands them and his direction is the one that serves the king. The verse is a study in how quickly the momentum of a cause absorbs even an atrocity committed in its name: the pursuit of the rebel Sheba covers, and in a sense excuses, the murder of the loyal Amasa, and the machine of war grinds on. What the historian records without comment, the commentators feel keenly, that justice deferred here is justice the army has already, by its silence and its onward motion, half consented to forget. Joab, unpunished as after Abner, leads them north. The verse also quietly answers the strategic worry of the previous verses: the pursuit that Amasa's delay had endangered is now, at the price of his life, back in motion under a general who will not hesitate. The kingdom's immediate safety is served, but by a hand David can neither trust nor control, so that even the recovery of the campaign carries within it the seed of the king's long humiliation before his own commander.
14:
Sheba's flight to the far north and the gathering of his last supporters occupy this verse, and the witnesses begin with a small grammatical crux before turning to the geography and the moral. John Gill notes that most read the subject as Sheba, who "passing through various tribes" to raise support and, "finding himself pursued," fled "from place to place," though "Josephus understands this of Joab pursuing Sheba," a reading followed by "the Syriac and Arabic versions"; Gill judges "the first sense is more generally received." John Wesley agrees it is Sheba who "marched from tribe to tribe to stir them up to sedition."

The grammatical-historical voices fix the place. Adam Clarke takes Abel for "the capital of the district called Abilene in St. Luke's Gospel" (Luke 3:1), with Beth-maachah "in the northern part of the Holy Land, on the confines of Syria." Charles Ellicott identifies Abel with "the modern Christian village of Abil," notes it is called "Abel-Beth-maachah" (1 Kings 15:20; 2 Kings 15:29) and "Abel-maim" (2 Chron. 16:4), "at the extreme north of the land," and admits "the Berites" are "apparently a family, or clan, in the north of Israel, otherwise entirely unknown." Wesley, following the Hebrew, takes them for men "of Beeroth of Benjamin" (Josh. 18:25), "of the same tribe... with Sheba," who "adhered to him."

Matthew Henry supplies the moral arc. The rebel, having "rambled over all the tribes" and found them cooler on reflection than in their first heat, "picked up a few like himself" and took shelter in "a strong city in the north." Henry's epigram catches the sociology of sedition: "One bad man will find or make more." And he turns the coming siege into allegory: "Justly is that place attacked with all this fury which dares to harbour a traitor; nor will that heart fare better which indulges those rebellious lusts that will not have Christ to reign over them." The witnesses thus watch Sheba's rising collapse to a handful of clansmen behind a northern wall, its whole momentum spent, awaiting only the arrival of Joab to end it.
15:
Joab's army comes up and lays siege, and the witnesses turn technical, disputing the exact nature of the siege-works. Keil and Delitzsch describe how the besiegers "piled up a rampart against the city, so that it rose up by the town-moat," the low wall and its ditch, "and all the people with Joab" laboured "to throw down the wall." John Wesley reads the "bank" plainly as a raised work "from whence they might either batter the wall, or shoot at those who defended it," standing "in, or near to the trench," so that "the city was in great danger of being taken."

Adam Clarke dissents on the word. The term rendered "bank," he argues, "means, most probably, a battering engine of some kind, or a tower overlooking the walls, on which archers and slingers could stand and annoy the inhabitants, while others of the besiegers could proceed to sap the walls." His reason is grammatical and vivid: "that it cannot be a bank that stood in the trench, is evident from the circumstance thus expressed." Charles Ellicott steers between them, keeping the "trench" as "the space between the wall of the city and the lower outer wall," and observing that "when the besiegers had succeeded in planting the mounds for their battering engines in this space they had already gained an important advantage." He also corrects the place-name, urging that the preposition be dropped: read "Abel-Beth-maachah," not "Abel of Beth-maachah."

The disagreement, though small, is characteristic of the grammatical-historical temper: whether the Hebrew pictures an earthen ramp or a wooden engine, all agree the city stood on the very edge of storm. The witnesses let the technical detail serve the narrative tension. Joab, having murdered his rival and resumed command, now throws the full weight of the royal army against a northern town for the sake of one fugitive within it. The wall is crumbling, the assault is minutes away, and the reader is braced for a massacre, precisely the point at which, in the next verse, an unnamed woman's voice rises from the doomed rampart to change the ending.
16:
Into the roar of the assault a single voice rises, and the witnesses greet the wise woman of Abel with unmistakable warmth. John Gill compares her to "the woman of Tekoah," supposes she may have been "the governess of the city," and grants that "whoever she was, she well deserved the character of a wise woman; her conduct in this affair shows it." He pictures her crying "with a loud voice, upon the wall of the city, to Joab's men, who were underneath battering it." Adam Clarke concurs in a line: "she was probably governess."

Keil and Delitzsch read forward to the proverb she will quote and draw the historical inference that "Abel had formerly been celebrated for the wisdom of its inhabitants" - a reputation the woman embodies and invokes. Her opening is all diplomatic economy: "Hear, hear; say, I pray you, unto Joab, Come near hither, that I may speak with thee." She will not shout terms across a battlefield; she summons the commander himself to a parley.

It is John Wesley who draws the fullest lesson, and it is one the historic church has loved to repeat. "It seems none of all the men of Abel offered to treat with Joab: no, not when they were reduced to extremity: but one wise woman saved the city." From this he strikes his often-quoted maxim: "Souls know no difference of sex: many a manly heart is lodged in a female breast. Nor is the treasure of wisdom the less valuable for being lodged in the weaker vessel." Matthew Henry, on the same scene, presses the identical point, marvelling that "this one woman and her wisdom saved the city" when the elders and magistrates stood "stupid and unconcerned," and concluding that "the treasure of wisdom" is "no less valuable for being lodged in the weaker vessel." The witnesses thus let the verse turn the whole tide of the chapter: where soldiers, generals, and city fathers have brought only bloodshed and paralysis, a nameless woman's wisdom opens the one door to peace, and the commentators honour her as an instrument of God's providence to a doomed town.
17:
The brief exchange of recognition, "Art thou Joab?... I am he," draws the witnesses to the woman's careful courtesy and to a nice point of Hebrew. John Gill notes only the natural sequence: Joab came near "upon the information of his men, that a woman on the wall had something to say to him." The commander, whose ruthlessness the earlier verses have exposed, is willing at least to hear.

Keil and Delitzsch attend to the grammar of her self-description, which she will unfold in the verses following. When she says "I am of the peaceable, faithful in Israel," the singular "I" is joined "with a predicate in the plural," and Keil explains that this "may be explained on the simple ground that the woman spoke in the name of the city as well as in its favour, and therefore had the citizens in her mind at the time." Her very syntax, that is, blurs the line between herself and her city; she speaks as one and as many, an advocate who has so identified with the community that its cause and her own are grammatically inseparable. Keil notes the same identification in the figure of the "mother" she will use, standing "for mother-city or capital."

The witnesses make of this small verse a study in the art of persuasion. The woman does not begin with demands or defiance; she begins by confirming the identity of the man she must move, securing his attention and his ear, "Hear the words of thine handmaid," to which the hardened Joab answers, "I do hear." Everything in her approach is measured: the summons, the courteous address, the patient establishing of a footing for parley. Where Joab has won his ends by the sword and the false embrace, the woman will win hers by wisdom and words, and the contrast is the point. The commentators, reading her opening moves, see already the shape of a bloodless victory taking form on the wall of a city that a moment before was doomed, and they admire the skill by which one voice bends a general who has just cut down a general.
18:
The woman opens her plea with a proverb, and the witnesses divide, gently, over what it once meant. John Gill takes it as "a common saying, a proverbial expression among the ancient sages." Adam Clarke confesses the difficulty honestly: "this is a proverb, but from what it originated we know not; nor can we exactly say what it means: much must be supplied to bring it to speak sense." His best reconstruction is that "Abel was probably famed for the wisdom of its inhabitants," so that "parties who had disputes appealed to their judgment," which was "final by consent of all parties"; and he hears in her words a rebuke, that Joab "should have proceeded in this way before he began to storm the city."

Charles Ellicott prefers the plainest reading, "that Abel had become proverbial for its wisdom," but records a second, ancient interpretation "incorporated into the Targum" and worth mentioning: "Remember now that which is written in the book of the Law to ask a city concerning peace at the first? Hast thou done so, to ask of Abel if they will make peace?" This ties the proverb to the law of siege in Deut. 20:10, which required Israel to "proclaim peace" to a city before assaulting it. John Wesley follows the first sense, expanding it: the city was "so honourable and considerable for its wisdom" that "when any differences arose... they used proverbially to say, We will ask the opinion and advice of the men of Abel," and "so all parties were satisfied, and disputes ended."

The disagreement is real but friendly. Is the proverb simply a boast of Abel's ancient renown for counsel (Ellicott's "simplest and most obvious explanation," with Gill and Wesley), or is it a pointed reminder of the Mosaic duty to offer terms before war (the Targum's reading, which Clarke's rebuke echoes)? Either way the witnesses agree on the woman's rhetorical aim: she claims for her city a dignity that ought to have been consulted, not besieged, and shames the general for storming a place renowned for settling quarrels by wisdom rather than by the sword. She meets Joab's violence, that is, with the memory of a better way, and invites him to end the dispute as Abel has always ended disputes, by asking and answering, not by battering down the wall.
19:
The woman presses her case with three pleas, and the witnesses draw each one out. John Gill hears in "I am one of them that are peaceable and faithful in Israel" a claim made on behalf of the whole city, "a city which consisted of peaceable and faithful men, that were peaceable among themselves, and faithful to their king, who never were concerned in any insurrection or rebellion, not in the late one under Absalom." Adam Clarke reads it the same way, that "the woman speaks... on behalf of the city": "I am a peaceable city, and am faithful to the king," and he takes "a mother in Israel" to mean "a chief city of a district," not the woman herself.

John Wesley unfolds the maternal figure most fully. "Great cities are commonly called mothers," he explains, "as lesser towns or villages subject to them, and depending upon them, are called their daughters." So Abel pleads not merely for its own walls but as a nursing centre for the towns around it. And Wesley gives full weight to the last and highest plea, "why wilt thou swallow up the inheritance of the LORD?": the city is "a considerable part of that land which God hath chosen for his particular possession," so that "the destruction which thou art about to bring upon us, is an injury to Israel, and to the God of Israel."

The witnesses see the woman's argument mounting in dignity, from the political to the theological. First she pleads innocence: Abel is loyal, no nest of rebels. Then she pleads worth: it is a mother-city, a source of life to its region, not a thing to be lightly swallowed up. Finally she pleads sacrilege: to destroy it is to lay hands on "the inheritance of the LORD," to diminish the very heritage God had chosen for his own. Matthew Henry, surveying the same speech, admired how "very ingeniously" she reasoned, that the town was "a part of the inheritance of the Lord... and the destruction of it would lessen and weaken that nation which God had chosen for his heritage." The commentators thus present her as more than an advocate for one town: she reminds the king's general that Israel's cities are God's, and that to waste them needlessly is not merely bad policy but an affront to heaven.
20:
Joab's reply, "Far be it, far be it from me, that I should swallow up or destroy," strikes the witnesses as revealing, and they read his character in it. John Gill notes that "he repeats these words, to show how detestable it was to him to do what she suggested," a strong disclaimer of any wish to raze the city. Keil and Delitzsch observe that "the woman's words made an impression upon Joab": "he felt the truthfulness of her reproaches," and answered with what is, in the Hebrew, an oath formula, "Far be it, far be it from me."

Charles Ellicott presses the point into a full portrait, drawing on the Speaker's Commentary. Joab "strongly disclaims the idea of any further harm to any one than the necessary destruction of the rebel Sheba," and Ellicott lets the older commentary sum up the man: his character "is strongly brought out in the transaction. Politic, decided, bold, and unscrupulous, but never needlessly cruel or impulsive, or even revengeful. No life is safe that stands in his way, but from policy he never sacrifices the most insignificant life without a purpose."

The witnesses thus catch the paradox that makes Joab so unsettling a figure. The same man who, a few verses earlier, ripped open his unsuspecting cousin under cover of a kiss, now recoils, apparently in sincerity, from the thought of destroying an innocent city. There is no contradiction in his own logic: Amasa stood in his way and had to die; Abel does not, and so may live. His cruelty is never wanton and his mercy is never soft; both are exactly calculated to his advantage. The commentators leave the reader to weigh how much more dangerous such a man is than a mere brute, precisely because he is reasonable, restrained, and always in command of himself. When Joab spares, it is not that his conscience has awakened but that his interest is served, and the wise woman, who has offered him a way to win without a costly assault, has read that interest exactly. Gill's emphasis on the doubled disclaimer and Keil's on the felt "truthfulness of her reproaches" need not be set against Ellicott's harder portrait; taken together they show a man who can be genuinely moved by a just word and yet moved only so far as it costs him nothing, whose relentings and whose ruthlessness alike bend to the single rule of what will keep him impregnable in his command.
21:
Joab names his one condition, and a bargain is struck, and the witnesses examine both the terms and the woman's remarkable authority. John Gill lets Joab explain that he came "not with any ill will to it, and with an intention to destroy it," but only for a single rebel. Keil and Delitzsch give the exchange in full: "a man of the mountains of Ephraim... Sheba the son of Bichri, hath lifted up his hand against the king David. Only give him up, and I will draw away from the city," to which "the woman promised him this: Behold, his head shall be thrown out to thee over the wall."

The commentators pause over "Mount Ephraim," since Sheba is a Benjamite. Charles Ellicott explains it is "the range of hills so called because much of it lay in the tribe of Ephraim, although extending south into Benjamin," and Keil likewise notes it "extended into the tribe of Benjamin." John Wesley agrees the name marks "a place in Benjamin," on the Ephraimite border. On the grim promise of the head, Adam Clarke draws two inferences: "thus it appears she had great sway in the counsels of the city," and that "the punishment of a state rebel was then, what it is now in this kingdom, beheading." Wesley presses the first point into a striking observation about her office: she undertook to deliver the head "because she knew the present temper of the citizens," and "it is not unlikely that this woman might be a governess in that city. For though this office was commonly performed by men, yet women were sometimes employed in the government: as we see in Deborah, who judged Israel" (Judg. 4:4).

Here the historic church has heard its favourite application, most fully in Matthew Henry: "The single condition of peace is the surrender of the traitor. It is so in God's dealing with the soul, when it is besieged by conviction and distress: sin is the traitor; the beloved lust is the rebel; part with that, cast away the transgression, and all shall be well. No peace on any other terms." The witnesses thus lift the negotiation into a parable of repentance. As Abel could be spared only by giving up the one rebel it sheltered, so the sinner, besieged by God's convicting mercy, finds peace on one condition alone, the surrender of the cherished sin. The wise woman, in agreeing to Joab's single demand, becomes an unwitting figure of the soul that saves itself by casting the traitor over the wall.
22:
The bargain is kept, the siege is lifted, and the witnesses close the episode by honouring the wisdom that achieved it. John Gill pictures the woman going "from the wall of the city into it," convening "the principal inhabitants together," and making "so wise a speech... and so prudently conducted, that they unanimously agreed to her motion." Keil and Delitzsch render the Hebrew's telling phrase: she came to the people "with her wisdom," that is, "with the wise counsel which she had given to Joab, and which he had accepted; whereupon the citizens cut off Sheba's head, and threw it out to Joab. Then Joab had a trumpet blown for a retreat, and the men disbanded, whilst he himself returned to Jerusalem to the king." John Wesley adds that she "prudently treated with them," "representing to them the certainty and nearness of their ruin" if they refused, "and certain deliverance if they did."

Charles Ellicott notes the homely idiom of the ending, "every man to his tent" meaning simply "to his home," echoing the very cry with which the chapter opened (v. 1), so that the rebellion which began with a summons to the tents ends with the army peaceably dismissed to theirs. Matthew Henry gathers the whole scene into its lasting lessons: "A great deal of mischief would be prevented if contending parties would but understand one another"; "Joab furiously attacks it, believing the citizens all confederates with Sheba. Whereas both were mistaken; let both sides be undeceived, and the matter is soon accommodated." And he praises the woman one last time, that "wisdom is not confined to rank or sex... but consists in understanding how to act as matters arise, that troubles may be turned away and benefits secured."

So the witnesses let the wise woman have the victory. A siege that threatened massacre is dissolved by a single head thrown over a wall; a general is satisfied, a city saved, and a king's peace restored, all through the counsel of a nameless woman who understood both her enemy and her neighbours. Where the men of war brought bloodshed, her wisdom brought deliverance, and the commentators send the reader away with Henry's quiet moral that most quarrels are prolonged by misunderstanding, and that a word of true wisdom, spoken in time, can turn away more ruin than an army can inflict.
23:
The chapter closes, as the first great section of David's reign closed, with a roster of his chief officers, and the witnesses begin with the loaded fact that "Joab was over all the host of Israel." John Gill weighs whether Joab had ever truly been displaced: either "he was never displaced," David being "not able to effect it, because of his power and influence with the people," or if he had been, "Amasa being dead, and the rebellion crushed by Joab," David "saw it most advisable to replace him." Adam Clarke is blunter: "he had murdered Amasa, and seized on the supreme command," and "David was obliged to continue him, and dared not to call him to account for his murders without endangering the safety of the state by a civil war." John Wesley agrees that "the good success of this, and of the former expedition... had so fixed his interest in the army... that the king could not without danger displace him."

Keil and Delitzsch lift the eye from Joab to the list itself, explaining its function: "the second section of the history of David's reign closes, like the first (2 Sam. 8:16), with a list of the leading ministers of state." The author, they note, preserved "the two lists" because "they belonged to different periods," and this one "belongs to a later period of David's reign," adding new offices, "Adoram over the tribute, and Ira the Kairite a confidential counsellor," where the earlier list had David's sons.

Matthew Henry reads the roster as "the state of David's court after his restoration," observing that "Joab retained the office of general, being too great to be displaced," and that among the officers "one new office" appears, "that of treasurer, or one over the tribute." Henry ends on the chapter's pastoral note: "It is well when able men are appointed to discharge public duties; let all seek to perform those duties, as faithful servants to the Son of David." The witnesses thus let the closing list quietly register both a triumph and a defeat: the rebellion is over and the kingdom ordered afresh, yet at its head stands a murderer the king cannot remove, a standing monument to the limits of David's power over the very men who serve him.
24:
The list continues with two officers, and the witnesses fasten on the first as a sign of the times, "Adoram was over the tribute." John Gill marks it "a new office," one that "respected both the revenue he received from his own people, and the tribute brought him from the nations conquered by him." Adam Clarke modernizes the post: Adoram was "probably the chief receiver of the taxes; or Chancellor of the Exchequer, as we term it," while Jehoshaphat the recorder was "the registrar of public events."

Charles Ellicott develops the point most fully and most tellingly. The same office, he notes, "was held by Adoniram in Solomon's reign" (1 Kings 4:6) "and by Adoram at the beginning of the reign of his successor" (1 Kings 12:18), so that "all those may have been the same person, or at least of the same family." More significant is his correction of the word: "'The tribute' should rather be the levy, the forced labour so largely employed by Solomon." And he draws the historical inference: "It is remarkable that there is no trace of such an office" in the earlier list of 2 Sam. 8:16; "it was a feature of Oriental despotism only introduced towards the close of David's reign, and carried to much greater length under Solomon."

The witnesses thus make the appearance of Adoram more than an administrative note. The very fact that David's court now needs an officer over the levy marks a change in the character of the monarchy: what had begun as the rule of a shepherd-king over a covenant people is beginning to take on the machinery of the surrounding despotisms, with taxation and forced labour as instruments of state. Keil, too, remarks that this later list adds "Adoram over the tribute" where the earlier had David's own sons, a shift the commentators feel is not merely bureaucratic but ominous. The office that here makes its quiet first appearance will, under Solomon, grow heavy enough to provoke a rebellion, and the man who holds it, or his namesake, will one day be stoned by an angry Israel for the burden it came to represent.
25:
The roster names two more, "Sheva was scribe; and Zadok and Abiathar were the priests," and the witnesses treat the verse mainly by comparison with the earlier list. John Gill weighs the identity of the secretary, suggesting Sheva was "either the same with" the officer of the first list, "or he was dead or removed, and Sheva was put in his place." Adam Clarke glosses the post plainly: "the king's secretary." Charles Ellicott supplies the textual link that most exercises the commentators: "this officer is called Seraiah in 2 Sam. 8:17," and he adds the general observation that "nearly all the officers mentioned here are the same as in 2 Sam. 8:16," where his fuller notes are to be found.

The variation in the scribe's name, Sheva here, Seraiah there (and Shavsha elsewhere), the witnesses take as a small instance of the way the same official could be recorded under differing forms, or the way one man succeeded another in a continuing office. Keil, comparing the lists, judged that they "belonged to different periods" and "supplemented one another," so that the reappearance of the priests Zadok and Abiathar alongside a differently named scribe reflects the passage of years rather than any contradiction.

Brief as it is, the verse carries its own quiet weight for the historic church. The presence of both Zadok and Abiathar as priests marks the continuity of Israel's worship through all the upheavals of rebellion and return; the king's realm is not only an army and a treasury but a covenant people with an altar at its centre. The commentators, reading this closing catalogue, see in it the restoration of ordered government, civil, military, and sacred, after the chaos of the two revolts. That the scribe's name has changed while the priests remain is itself a small parable: administrators come and go, but the ministry of the sanctuary abides, a fixed point around which the shifting offices of the state revolve, and a reminder that David's throne rested, in the end, upon the God whose priests still stood before him. The witnesses are content to leave the small textual puzzle of the scribe's name unresolved, for it touches nothing of substance; what matters to them is the double witness of the two lists, that through years of triumph and near-ruin the essential ministries of Israel, the sword, the pen, and above all the altar, were kept in faithful hands, so that the covenant people emerged from its convulsions with its worship intact and its government whole.
26:
The chapter ends with a single officer whose title puzzles the witnesses, "Ira also the Jairite was a chief ruler about David," and the debate is worth the close. John Gill canvasses the various identifications of the man himself, wondering whether he was "Ira the Ithrite," or "Ira the son of Ikkesh the Tekoite," or "a descendant from Jair the Gileadite," perhaps "a great friend to David when in Gilead." Charles Ellicott is more cautious, noting he "is not mentioned in the other lists," and that though an Ira appears among David's heroes (2 Sam. 23:38), "there is no ground for identifying the two persons."

The real crux is the office, and here the witnesses divide over a single Hebrew word. Adam Clarke presses its literal force: "the Hebrew is cohen ledavid, a priest to David; and so the Vulgate, Septuagint, Syriac, and Arabic," though "the Chaldee has rab, a prince, or chief." Clarke leans to the former, guessing Ira "was probably a sort of domestic chaplain to the king," and compares the seers the kings kept about them, "Gad was David's seer" (2 Sam. 24:11). Ellicott keeps the term untranslated as "cohen" and supplies the historical background that explains it: "earlier in David's reign the office had been occupied by his own sons, but the murder of the eldest, the rebellion and death of Absalom, and other disorders in his household had led apparently to a change."

So the two readings stand side by side: was Ira a kind of personal priest or chaplain to the king (Clarke, with the ancient versions), or a chief civil counsellor, a "prince" in the royal household (the Chaldee's rab)? The witnesses do not force a verdict, but Ellicott's note gives the office its poignancy. Where once David's own sons had held this place of nearness to the throne, now, after the sword had done its work in his family, a stranger fills it. The chapter that opened with rebellion and ran through murder and siege closes on this muted domestic sorrow: the king's household reordered by grief, the sons who should have stood beside him dead or disgraced, and their place taken by Ira the Jairite. The historic church reads in that final name both the mercy that gave David faithful servants to the last and the shadow of the judgement, foretold long before, that the sword would not depart from his house.
